title: Blow Up or Shut Down: Two Sides of the Same Stress Response
series_subtitle: A Field Guide for Frontline Folks and Their Families
cover_tagline: Fight and freeze are both stress responses. Learn to catch yellow before red.

sections.heading: Two Stress Modes, One Nervous System

content.text: Some days your stress response comes out as intensity, volume, and anger. Other days it comes out as numbness, silence, and emotional disappearance. Both are protective states, not moral failures.

items: you push to control the environment
you go flat, quiet, and hard to reach
you avoid eye contact and conversation
you disconnect to avoid making it worse

sections.heading: Your Yellow Zone Is the Intervention Window

content.text: Red is expensive. Yellow is workable. If you can detect yellow, you can prevent both blowups and shutdowns.

lines: You can listen, think, and respond.
Body tension rises, thinking gets rigid, tone shifts.
You are likely to damage trust if you continue.

sections.heading: Practical Interrupts for Blow Up and Shut Down

items.text: say, 'I am getting loaded,' before your body chooses for you.
walk, breathe, cold water, or wall push for 60-120 seconds.
never pause without saying when you will re-engage.
apology plus behavior change restores safety faster than silence.

items: Use short phrases: 'You are yellow. Want 20 minutes?'
Do not chase someone in shutdown with rapid-fire questions.
After red, schedule a repair conversation with one clear objective.

sections.heading: When This Pattern Needs Clinical Support

items: Frequent verbal aggression, intimidation, or emotional disappearance.
Family members are modifying life to avoid your reactions.
Shame cycles are increasing substance use or hopelessness.
Repair attempts are not changing outcomes over several weeks.

content.text: At end of each day, mark your highest zone, what moved you there, and whether you used a yellow intervention in time.

worksheets.title: Worksheet: Blow Up or Shut Down - Pattern Tracker
worksheets.intro: Map whether your stress response went explosive, avoidant, or both, and what worked to interrupt it.

question_groups.label: When did you move toward blow up this week?
question_groups.hint: List the setting, trigger, and first warning cue.

question_groups.label: When did you move toward shut down this week?
question_groups.hint: List where you disconnected, went silent, or emotionally disappeared.

question_groups.label: What yellow intervention will you use next time?
question_groups.hint: Choose one action and one phrase to deploy before red.

numbered_prompts.hint: What shifted you from green to yellow?
Heat, tight jaw, shallow breath, numbness, tunnel hearing, etc.
What interrupted escalation in under two minutes?
If damage occurred, what exact repair will you do?

worksheets.closing_note: Success is not never going yellow. Success is fewer reds and faster repairs.

worksheets.title: Worksheet: Blow Up or Shut Down - Shared Safety Plan
worksheets.intro: Use this with partner or family to define what yellow and red look like and what each person will do.

question_groups.label: Observable yellow signs
question_groups.hint: List behaviors each person can reliably identify.

question_groups.label: Red protocol
question_groups.hint: What happens immediately when either person calls red?

question_groups.label: Repair protocol
question_groups.hint: How and when will you complete repair after red?

numbered_prompts.hint: Short phrase both people agree to honor.
Exact time to reconnect after pause.
Write the first line you will use in repair conversation.

worksheets.closing_note: A shared protocol turns chaos into a plan.

paragraphs: Blow up and shut down are not opposite personalities. They are different exits from the same overloaded nervous system.
The skill is not perfection. The skill is naming yellow, pausing early, and repairing fast.
closing.highlight: Call yellow early enough and you can protect the people you love from your red.
